summary: date-uuid ships an import-time dropper that decrypts a hidden URL, fetches a VBS payload, and executes it
details: On require()/import of date-uuid@1.0.3, lib/bootstrap.js invokes extractDateISO(), which retrieves a remote URL by AES-256-GCM-decrypting ciphertext read from lines 120 and 123 of the host project's README using a hardcoded key/IV/authTag. The package then POSTs the installer's OS platform and release (os.platform() + os.release()) to that endpoint, downloads the response, writes it to os.tmpdir() as temp_<timestamp>.vbs (extension obfuscated by string concatenation 'v'+'b'+'s'), executes it via child_process.exec, and deletes it. The package's advertised purpose is generating UUID v7 strings; the fetch-decrypt-execute path is unrelated to UUID generation. Multiple obfuscation techniques (hardcoded AES key/IV/authTag, ciphertext sourced from an external README to keep the package's own files looking benign, split-concatenated file extension) confirm intent to evade static analysis. The name resembles legitimate UUID/date utilities, increasing the chance of mistaken installs.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
